text: The surgeon divides the gastrocolic ligament to mobilize the transverse colon inferiorly, separating attachments to the gallbladder and liver to expose the duodenum.

The surgeon initiates the Kocher maneuver by dividing the retroperitoneal attachments to the anti-mesenteric border of the duodenum. The robotic arm with tip-up capability is used to retract the duodenum, providing exposure for precise division along the anatomical planes.
The intraoperative ultrasound image reveals the ampulla, with a visible stent placed within the distal common bile duct.

The surgeon places stay sutures on either side of the planned duodenotomy using V-lock suture. The sutures are secured to the abdominal wall on both sides of the patient's abdomen to provide counter traction, visibly stabilizing the duodenum for the incision.
The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to create a duodenotomy, successfully identifying the ampulla and the adenoma within the duodenum.

The surgeon places a V-lock suture through the superior aspect of the ampulla for retraction, delivering the adenoma into the surgical field. The ampulla and duodenum are exposed, and hook electrocautery is used to initiate resection through normal-appearing duodenal and ampullary tissue.

The lumen of the bile duct is visualized, and the pancreatic duct is identified at the four to five o'clock position. The surgeon places the first V-lock suture to re-approximate the distal bile duct to the duodenum, ensuring the pancreatic duct remains unobstructed. A second V-lock suture is placed to approximate the superior bile duct to the duodenum.

The surgeon performs circumferential resection of the ampulla and adenoma, carefully freeing the specimen from surrounding tissue. Once fully detached, the specimen is placed into an endocatch bag for transport to pathology.

The surgeon uses the previously placed superior V-lock suture to approximate the distal bile duct to the duodenum in a running fashion, completing the circumferential anastomosis. The suture is then tied to the initially placed V-lock suture, securing the connection.

The surgeon removes the biliary stent and proceeds to close the vertical duodenotomy horizontally using previously placed V-lock stay sutures. A canal stitch is applied to close the duodenotomy in a single layer from each corner.
